summary: 3b completes the 'kach lecha'/'aseh lecha' dispute over personal vs. communal property. R. Yoshiya holds both phrases mean personal property; R. Yonatan holds both are communal but God expresses a preference for Moses's personal contribution. Abba Chanan resolves the Ark contradiction not by grammar but by context - when Israel obeys God, the work is credited to them. The Gemara then shows that regardless of the general rule, the specific inauguration and YK passages contain explicit textual contrasts that prove personal property. Rav Ashi adds an independent exclusion: derive 'bull-chatat + ram-olah' from 'bull-chatat + ram-olah,' excluding RH and Shavuot where both offerings are olot. Ravina provides the most elegant solution: derive High Priest service from High Priest service. Rav Dimi reports that R. Yochanan derived only one case (YK) from the inauguration, while R. Yehoshua b. Levi derived two (heifer + YK). R. Yochanan treats heifer sequestering as purely rabbinic (a maaleh). Reish Lakish challenges the inauguration derivation - if YK is derived from inauguration, all inauguration details should be binding. R. Yochanan deflects: the mishna uses 'matkin' (designate) not 'mafrishin' (remove) for the backup priest, showing sequestering is not indispensable. Reish Lakish reveals his own source: Sinai - the six days Moses was covered by cloud. The page ends with the obvious challenge: our mishna requires seven days, not six.

display.title: Kach lecha and Aseh lecha: personal or communal - the dispute and its resolution
display.oneLine: R. Yoshiya says both 'take for yourself' and 'make for yourself' mean personal property; R. Yonatan says both are communal but God prefers Moses's contribution. Abba Chanan resolves the Ark contradiction by context. The Gemara then overrides the general dispute: both inauguration and YK passages explicitly signal personal property through textual contrast.
display.shortSummary: The tannaitic dispute over 'kach lecha'/'aseh lecha' does not definitively resolve which interpretation is correct. Abba Chanan cuts through it: when Israel obeys God they get credit, when not, Moses acts alone - no property distinction needed. But the Gemara shows the dispute is moot here anyway: both key passages explicitly contrast communal language with a special personal-property formulation, proving the offerings must come from the Kohen Gadol's personal funds regardless of the general rule.
display.whats: The daf opens mid-discussion. R. Abbahu had argued on 3a that 'kach lecha' proves the YK and inauguration offerings come from the Kohen's personal funds. The problem: R. Yonatan holds 'kach lecha' actually signals communal property - so R. Abbahu's argument fails. A baraita presents the dispute: R. Yoshiya says 'kach lecha' and 'aseh lecha' both mean personal; R. Yonatan says both phrases mean communal, but God expresses a preference for Moses's personal contribution ('I desire yours more than theirs'). Abba Chanan resolves a Tanach contradiction about the Ark (Deut 10:1 says 'make you an ark,' Exod 25:10 says 'they shall make an ark') not by the kach/aseh grammar but by context: when Israel obeys, they get credit; when not, Moses acts alone. The Gemara then sidesteps the whole dispute: in both the inauguration and YK passages, the Torah itself creates an explicit contrast - addressing Israel with one phrase and Aaron with 'kach lecha' (inauguration) or 'his bull' (YK). That contrast, not the general rule, proves personal property in each case.
display.hint: Abba Chanan's contextual reading bypasses the grammatical dispute entirely. What does this say about how the Talmud adjudicates between competing hermeneutical methods?

learning.learnerQuestion: R. Abbahu's argument depended on 'kach lecha' meaning personal property - but R. Yonatan says it actually means communal. Doesn't that destroy the derivation?
learning.coreTension: The exclusion of Rosh Hashana seemed to rest on a grammatical rule about 'kach lecha.' But that rule is itself contested. If the derivation depends on a disputed reading, it is on shaky ground.
learning.coreMove: Bypass the general dispute by identifying explicit textual contrasts in the specific passages: each passage itself distinguishes between communal and personal property, making the general grammatical rule irrelevant to these cases.
learning.resolution: Both the inauguration and YK passages create their own internal proof of personal property through explicit contrast - Israel is addressed one way, the Kohen Gadol another. The tannaitic dispute about the general rule does not touch these specific passages.

takeaway.text: When a general hermeneutical rule is contested, look for a textual proof internal to the specific passage. A verse that explicitly contrasts two groups' obligations does not need an external rule to interpret it.

reasoningPattern.notes: The Gemara overrides a disputed general rule with a specific textual contrast that is self-proving.

learning.ahaMoment: The whole 'kach lecha' dispute becomes irrelevant because the verse in each case creates its own contrast: 'tell Israel to take a goat kid... and tell Aaron: take for yourself a calf.' The contrast is the proof - no general rule needed.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may think the Gemara must resolve the tannaitic dispute before proceeding. Instead it shows the dispute does not apply to these passages.
learning.memoryAnchor: Both passages contrast a communal command with a personal one within the same verse. The contrast is the argument - it works even for the view that 'kach lecha' generally means communal.
alternateAngles: Abba Chanan's contextual reading of the Ark contradiction is actually a different approach entirely - he abandons the grammatical question and asks: when does God credit Israel with shared work? Answer: when they are obeying. This is a theology of collaborative merit, not a property rule.

argumentFlow.label: R. Yonatan: 'kach lecha' is communal
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The exclusion of RH and Shavuot was based on 'kach lecha' meaning personal property. But R. Yonatan says 'kach lecha' means communal - God merely prefers Moses's contribution.

argumentFlow.label: Baraita: R. Yoshiya vs. R. Yonatan
argumentFlow.speaker: Tanna
argumentFlow.text: R. Yoshiya: 'kach lecha' = personal; 'veyikchu eilecha' = communal. R. Yonatan: both mean communal; 'kach lecha' is God saying 'I prefer yours more than theirs.'

argumentFlow.label: Abba Chanan: context, not grammar
argumentFlow.speaker: Abba Chanan (via R. Elazar)
argumentFlow.text: The Ark contradiction (Deut 10:1 vs. Exod 25:10) is resolved not by grammar but by Israel's spiritual state: when they obey, they get credit; when they don't, Moses acts alone.

argumentFlow.label: The specific passages prove their own point
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The general kach/aseh dispute applies to ordinary occurrences. But the inauguration verse itself contrasts Israel's command with Aaron's 'kach lecha' - the contrast internally proves personal property without needing the general rule.

sourceRefs.note: The Gemara's rejection of the dispute's relevance here

conceptRefs.label: Communal vs. personal property in Temple offerings

misconceptions.misconception: The tannaitic dispute over 'kach lecha' must be resolved before the argument can proceed.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara shows the dispute is irrelevant to these specific cases. Both passages create their own internal proof of personal property through explicit textual contrast - independent of any general rule about 'kach lecha.'

visualizableElements.item: Two parallel verse columns - inauguration verse showing Israel's command vs. Aaron's 'kach lecha,' YK verse showing 'from the community' vs. 'his bull' - the contrast visible side-by-side
Abba Chanan's ark resolution: two verses side by side (Deut 10:1 singular, Exod 25:10 plural) with arrow pointing to 'when Israel obeys vs. not'

quizSeeds.question: R. Yonatan says 'kach lecha' means communal property - God just prefers Moses's contribution. How does this threaten R. Abbahu's exclusion of Rosh Hashana?
quizSeeds.answer: R. Abbahu had derived that the YK/inauguration offerings must be personal because of the 'kach lecha' phrase. If R. Yonatan is right that 'kach lecha' is actually communal, then this phrase proves nothing about personal property - and Rosh Hashana cannot be excluded on that basis.

quizSeeds.question: How does the Gemara salvage the personal-property requirement for the inauguration and YK offerings despite the tannaitic dispute?
quizSeeds.answer: By finding explicit textual contrasts within each passage: the inauguration verse addresses Israel communally and then addresses Aaron with 'kach lecha' - the contrast itself proves Aaron must use personal funds. Similarly for YK. This proof works regardless of the general 'kach lecha' rule.

sugyot.finalRuling: The bull and ram on Yom Kippur and at the inauguration must come from the Kohen Gadol's personal funds - proven by explicit textual contrast within each passage, independent of the disputed 'kach lecha' rule.

display.title: Explicit derivation for Yom Kippur; Rav Ashi and Ravina's independent exclusions
display.oneLine: For YK, 'his bull' in the same verse as 'from the community' proves personal property. Rav Ashi adds: derive bull-chatat + ram-olah from bull-chatat + ram-olah. Ravina: derive High Priest service from High Priest service. Ravina's alternative: initial service in the holy place from initial service in the holy place.
display.shortSummary: Yom Kippur's explicit proof: Leviticus 16 mentions the community's goats and then adds 'his bull' (the contrast implies personal). Rav Ashi offers a structural exclusion: the inauguration and YK share a bull-chatat + ram-olah pair; RH and Shavuot don't - excluding them. Ravina's first version: only High Priest-restricted service can derive from inauguration-level service. Ravina's second version: initial service in the sanctuary from initial service in the sanctuary.
display.whats: Yom Kippur's proof: Leviticus 16 commands the KG to take two goats 'from the community' (for the people) and then says 'he shall bring his bull' - the word 'his' in the same verse as 'from the community' proves personal ownership. Rav Ashi offers another approach: derive from the structural matching of offering types: inauguration and YK both involve a bull-chatat + ram-olah combination. Rosh Hashana and Shavuot bring only olot (no chatat) - so they cannot be derived from the inauguration's chatat-olah pattern. Ravina (version 1): derive KG-restricted service from KG-restricted service. Since RH and Shavuot are not KG-exclusive services, none of the questions raised apply. Ravina (version 2): 'initial service in the place' - the Holy of Holies was entered for the first time on the eighth day and on Yom Kippur. This is the deepest criterion: it filters to these exact two occasions.
display.hint: Rav Ashi and Ravina offer independent arguments that work even without the personal-property derivation. What does the Gemara's preservation of all three approaches tell us?

learning.learnerQuestion: Why does the Gemara need three independent arguments (personal property, offering profile, High Priest service) when one would suffice?
learning.coreTension: Each argument has its own scope of exclusion - they don't all exclude exactly the same set of candidates. The question is whether they converge on the same correct answer by different paths.
learning.coreMove: Each authority closes a different remaining loophole. The three arguments form a converging system: any calendar candidate that survives one criterion is caught by another.
learning.resolution: All three approaches independently converge on the same answer: only Yom Kippur derives from the inauguration (for the Torah-level seven-day separation). They are preserved because each reveals a distinct dimension of why only YK qualifies.

takeaway.text: Multiple independent derivations pointing to the same conclusion strengthen the ruling without requiring each one to be individually decisive. The convergence itself is evidence.

reasoningPattern.notes: Three independent authorities each derive the same exclusion through different hermeneutical lenses. The convergence validates the ruling.

learning.ahaMoment: Ravina's 'initial service in the place' is the most precise criterion: it captures the uniqueness of both the eighth day and Yom Kippur (first services ever performed at their respective locations) without relying on offering profiles or priestly rank.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may wonder why three arguments are needed if one works. The Gemara preserves all three because each excludes different hypothetical extensions of the rule, and each illuminates a different dimension of what makes YK analogous to the inauguration.
learning.memoryAnchor: Three lenses on the same exclusion: (1) personal property - the bull is 'his'; (2) offering profile - bull-chatat + ram-olah, not two olot; (3) High Priest only service - KG from KG, or inaugural service from inaugural service.

argumentFlow.label: YK explicit: 'his bull' vs. 'from the community'
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Leviticus 16 commands taking two goats 'from the community of Israel' for the people, then says 'he shall bring his sin-offering bull.' The contrast within the verse proves the bull must be personal.

sourceRefs.note: Explicit proof for YK personal property

argumentFlow.label: Rav Ashi: offering profile
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav Ashi
argumentFlow.text: Derive bull-chatat + ram-olah (YK) from bull-chatat + ram-olah (inauguration), to the exclusion of Rosh Hashana and Shavuot - where both offerings are olot, not the chatat-olah pair.

argumentFlow.label: Ravina v.1: High Priest service from High Priest service
argumentFlow.speaker: Ravina
argumentFlow.text: Derive High Priest-restricted service (YK) from High Priest-restricted service (inauguration) - excluding all the other candidates, whose service may be performed by any kohen.

argumentFlow.label: Ravina v.2: initial service in the place
argumentFlow.speaker: Ravina (alternate version)
argumentFlow.text: Derive initial service in the sacred place (first entry into the Holy of Holies on YK) from initial service in the sacred place (first service at the Tabernacle on the eighth day) - the most precise criterion.

sourceRefs.note: Ravina's second version: first service in the place

misconceptions.misconception: The Gemara is being repetitive by giving three independent answers to the same question.
misconceptions.correction: Each argument excludes a slightly different set of hypothetical extensions. Rav Ashi's offering-profile argument would not have worked to exclude Shemini Atzeret (which has a similar offering); Ravina's 'KG service' and 'initial service in the place' arguments close different remaining gaps.

visualizableElements.item: Three-column comparison: YK offering profile (bull-chatat + ram-olah) vs. RH offering profile (both olot) vs. inauguration profile (bull-chatat + ram-olah) - showing which pairs match

Ravina's criterion: 'initial service in the place' - timeline showing eighth day of inauguration (first entry at the altar) and first Yom Kippur (first entry into the Holy of Holies) as the only two qualifying moments

quizSeeds.question: How does Rav Ashi's argument exclude Rosh Hashana from deriving from the inauguration?
quizSeeds.answer: The inauguration and YK both involve a bull-chatat + ram-olah combination. On Rosh Hashana, both the bull and ram are olot - no chatat. You can only derive 'bull-chatat + ram-olah' from 'bull-chatat + ram-olah,' so RH is excluded.

quizSeeds.question: What does Ravina's second version mean by 'initial service in the place'?
quizSeeds.answer: The Yom Kippur service involves entering the Holy of Holies - which on the first Yom Kippur was entered for the very first time. Similarly, the eighth day of the inauguration was the first service performed at the outer altar. Both are 'initial' services at their respective locations. This criterion uniquely captures both occasions.

sugyot.finalRuling: Yom Kippur is the sole calendar event whose seven-day separation requirement derives from the inauguration at Torah level, established by three independent arguments: personal property contrast, offering-profile matching, and High Priest/inaugural service restriction.

display.title: Rav Dimi's report: one case or two derived from the inauguration?
display.oneLine: R. Yochanan derives only Yom Kippur from 'to do, to atone'; R. Yehoshua b. Levi derives both heifer and YK. But the mishna explicitly mentions both separations - so R. Yochanan must hold heifer sequestering is only rabbinic (a maaleh).
display.shortSummary: Rav Dimi reports a disagreement between leading Amoraim: R. Yochanan reads 'to do, to atone' as referring only to YK; R. Yehoshua b. Levi reads it as referring to both heifer ('to do') and YK ('to atone'). The Gemara challenges R. Yochanan's one-case position with the mishna's explicit mention of heifer sequestering. R. Yochanan resolves this: heifer sequestering is rabbinic only. A further challenge from a transmission in R. Yochanan's own name teaching two cases is resolved: that was his teacher R. Yishmael's view, transmitted through R. Yochanan but not his own.
display.whats: Rav Dimi brings a report from Israel: R. Yochanan taught that the verse 'to do, to atone' derives only Yom Kippur from the inauguration; R. Yehoshua b. Levi taught it derives both the heifer and Yom Kippur. The Gemara immediately challenges R. Yochanan: the mishna itself lists heifer sequestering as equal to YK sequestering - doesn't that prove two cases? R. Yochanan's answer is terse: the heifer sequestering is a rabbinic enhancement (maaleh) without a Torah source. A further complication: a different chain of transmission quotes R. Yochanan himself as teaching two cases. Resolution: that transmission was in the name of his teacher R. Yishmael; when Ravin came from Israel, he clarified that R. Yochanan personally held only one case.
display.hint: R. Yochanan's position has a significant consequence: heifer sequestering is only rabbinic while YK sequestering is Torah-level. What practical difference could this make?

learning.learnerQuestion: Why does R. Yochanan insist on deriving only one case from the inauguration, when the mishna clearly mandates both?
learning.coreTension: R. Yochanan is committed to reading 'to do, to atone' as a single unified phrase referring to one event (YK), not two. But the mishna's explicit parallel - 'seven days before YK, seven days before the heifer' - seems to directly contradict his position.
learning.coreMove: Classify the heifer sequestering as rabbinic (maaleh) rather than Torah-derived. This keeps the derivation clean (one Torah source, one Torah-derived case) while accommodating the mishna as a rabbinic enactment.
learning.resolution: R. Yochanan: 'to do, to atone' = one case (YK). The heifer sequestering is a rabbinic stringency added because the Sages permitted a tvul yom to perform the heifer ritual. The apparent contradiction from his own transmitted ruling is resolved: that was his teacher's view.

takeaway.text: A rabbinic enactment (maaleh) may look identical to a Torah obligation in practice but differs in scope and overridability. Classifying a law as one vs. the other has real legal consequences.

reasoningPattern.notes: The same external practice (seven-day sequestering) is Torah-level for YK and rabbinic for the heifer. The distinction matters for edge cases where the full requirement cannot be met.

learning.ahaMoment: The transmission chain matters: what R. Yochanan cited in one context was his teacher R. Yishmael's ruling. When Ravin came from Israel, he clarified what R. Yochanan personally held. The Gemara carefully distinguishes between 'R. Yochanan said X' and 'R. Yochanan cited X in the name of R. Yishmael.'
learning.learningBlocker: Students may assume the mishna's listing of both cases settles the question in favor of two-case Torah derivation. R. Yochanan shows that mishna passages can reflect rabbinic requirements, not only Torah-derived ones.
learning.memoryAnchor: R. Yochanan reads one case from the verse but the mishna lists two. His bridge: the second case (heifer) is a rabbinic maaleh, not a Torah derivation. The verse teaches only YK; the Sages added the heifer as a compensating stringency for their tvul yom leniency.

argumentFlow.label: Rav Dimi's report
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav Dimi
argumentFlow.text: R. Yochanan taught one case: 'to do, to atone' = YK service only. R. Yehoshua b. Levi taught two cases: 'to do' = heifer, 'to atone' = YK.

sourceRefs.note: Rav Dimi's report from Eretz Yisrael

argumentFlow.label: The mishna lists both
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: How can R. Yochanan derive only one case? The mishna explicitly mentions both: seven days before YK and seven days before burning the heifer!

sourceRefs.note: Challenge from the mishna's dual listing

argumentFlow.label: Heifer sequestering is a maaleh only
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yochanan
argumentFlow.text: The heifer sequestering is a rabbinic stringency - a compensating measure for the leniency of permitting a tvul yom to perform the heifer service. The mishna records a rabbinic requirement, not a Torah-derived one.

argumentFlow.label: But R. Yochanan himself taught two cases
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: But didn't R. Minyomi bar Chilkiya cite a ruling from R. Yochanan explicitly deriving both heifer and YK from the verse 'to do, to atone'?

sourceRefs.note: Second challenge: R. Yochanan's own transmitted ruling

argumentFlow.label: That was his teacher R. Yishmael's view
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: That transmission was his teacher's opinion - R. Yishmael. When Ravin came from Israel, he clarified that R. Yochanan personally taught one case, in the name of R. Yishmael.

sourceRefs.note: Resolution: attributed to R. Yishmael via R. Yochanan

conceptRefs.label: Maaleh - rabbinic stringency beyond the law

misconceptions.misconception: If the mishna requires heifer sequestering, it must be Torah-level.
misconceptions.correction: R. Yochanan shows that the mishna can record a rabbinic enactment (maaleh) that looks identical to a Torah requirement in practice. Classification as Torah vs. rabbinic is not always visible in the mishna's language.

visualizableElements.item: Two-column table: R. Yochanan's reading (one case: YK) vs. R. Yehoshua b. Levi's reading (two cases: heifer + YK) - with the same verse text above both columns
Transmission chain diagram: R. Yishmael -> R. Yochanan (attributed) -> R. Minyomi bar Chilkiya (cited as two cases) vs. R. Yochanan's own position (one case) clarified by Ravin

quizSeeds.question: R. Yochanan holds the verse derives only one case from the inauguration. How does he explain the mishna's explicit mention of heifer sequestering?
quizSeeds.answer: Heifer sequestering is a maaleh - a rabbinic enhancement with no Torah source. The Sages added it as a compensating stringency because they permitted a tvul yom to burn the heifer (a leniency). The mishna records a rabbinic requirement, not a Torah-derived one.

quizSeeds.question: How is the apparent contradiction resolved between R. Yochanan citing one case and the transmission in his name citing two cases?
quizSeeds.answer: The two-case ruling in R. Yochanan's name was actually his teacher R. Yishmael's view, which R. Yochanan transmitted in his teacher's name but did not personally hold. Ravin's report from Eretz Yisrael clarifies R. Yochanan's own position: one case only.

alternateAngles: The distinction between R. Yochanan 'saying' something and R. Yochanan 'citing his teacher' is critical in Talmudic transmission. Ravin's report resolves the contradiction by clarifying what R. Yochanan personally held vs. what he attributed to R. Yishmael.

display.title: Reish Lakish challenges the inauguration derivation; proposes Sinai instead
display.oneLine: If YK derives from the inauguration, all inauguration details should invalidate YK if violated - but the mishna shows they don't. R. Yochanan deflects with the 'matkin' vs. 'mafrishin' argument. Reish Lakish proposes Sinai as his source: six days Moses was covered in cloud. The page ends with the challenge that our mishna says seven days, not six.
display.shortSummary: Reish Lakish presses a formal challenge: deriving from the inauguration would require all inauguration details to be indispensable. The mishna itself refutes this - the backup priest is 'designated' (matkin), not 'separated' (mafrishin), showing sequestering is not indispensable. Reish Lakish then reveals his alternative: Sinai, where Moses was covered in cloud for six days before God called him on the seventh. This gives a general principle - anyone entering the Divine Presence requires six days of preparation. But our mishna requires seven - and the daf ends open.
display.whats: Reish Lakish challenges R. Yochanan: if YK derives from the inauguration, then all the details written about the inauguration should invalidate the service if violated - including, perhaps, the backup priest needing to be separated too. But the mishna uses 'matkin' (designate, prepare) for the backup priest, not 'mafrishin' (separate). If sequestering were indispensable, the mishna would use 'mafrishin' for both the KG and the backup. The word difference proves sequestering is not invalidating. R. Yochanan turns the challenge back: so then where do YOU derive it from? Reish Lakish reveals his source: Moses at Sinai. The cloud covered Moses for six days before God called to him on the seventh - proving that anyone entering the Divine Presence needs six days of preparation. The challenge: our mishna says seven. Reish Lakish's answer (continuing on 4a): the mishna follows R. Yehuda b. Beteira, who requires seven out of concern for niddah impurity.
display.hint: The 'matkin' vs. 'mafrishin' argument is a close reading of a single word. What makes this kind of argument reliable evidence in Talmudic reasoning?

learning.learnerQuestion: If Reish Lakish rejects the inauguration as the source, isn't he left without any derivation for the seven-day rule?
learning.coreTension: Reish Lakish must reject the inauguration derivation because it creates an obligation to follow all inauguration details. But then he needs an alternative source. His Sinai derivation yields six days, not seven - immediately creating a new problem.
learning.coreMove: Sinai generates a general principle (six days for entering Divine Presence) that avoids the inauguration's over-specification problem. The gap to seven days is then filled by a tannaitic attribution.
learning.resolution: The page closes open. Reish Lakish answers the seven-vs-six challenge: the mishna follows R. Yehuda b. Beteira's concern about niddah impurity. This resolution continues on 4a.

takeaway.text: A derivation creates an analogy in both directions: it imports laws from the source case and also subjects the derived case to the source's full framework. This double obligation can itself become a reason to reject a derivation in favor of an alternative source.

reasoningPattern.notes: Reish Lakish's challenge shows that the inauguration analogy proves too much - it would make all inauguration details binding. This forces a search for an alternative source.

learning.ahaMoment: The 'matkin vs. mafrishin' argument is the key: a single word difference in the mishna is enough to prove that sequestering is not indispensable. If it were, the mishna would say 'they also separate the backup priest.' It doesn't - so sequestering is not invalidating.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may not see why deriving from Sinai avoids the overspecification problem. Sinai gives a general principle (entrance into Divine Presence requires preparation) without the full inauguration template. There's no list of Sinai-specific rules to import.
learning.memoryAnchor: Reish Lakish refuses to derive from the inauguration because it would import all inauguration details. His source is Sinai: six days, then the seventh day call. Clean, general, no side effects. The only problem: our mishna says seven, not six.

argumentFlow.label: All inauguration details should invalidate
argumentFlow.speaker: Reish Lakish
argumentFlow.text: You derive YK sequestering from the inauguration - but shouldn't that mean failure to observe any inauguration detail also invalidates the YK service?

sourceRefs.note: Reish Lakish's challenge: overspecification from inauguration

argumentFlow.label: Matkin vs. mafrishin refutes overspecification
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yochanan
argumentFlow.text: The mishna uses 'matkin' (designate) for the backup priest, not 'mafrishin' (remove). If sequestering were indispensable, the mishna would require separation for the backup too - the word choice proves it is not.

argumentFlow.label: R. Yochanan asks Reish Lakish's source
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yochanan
argumentFlow.text: You rejected my inauguration derivation - then from where do YOU derive the sequestering rule?

argumentFlow.label: Reish Lakish: from Sinai
argumentFlow.speaker: Reish Lakish
argumentFlow.text: From Sinai: 'The glory of the Lord rested on Mount Sinai, and the cloud covered it six days, and He called to Moses on the seventh day.' Since the verse tells us Moses was called on the seventh, the six days of cloud-covering teach that anyone entering the Divine Presence requires six days of preparation.

argumentFlow.label: But our mishna says seven!
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Our mishna requires seven days of sequestering - not six. How does Sinai's six days account for our mishna?

conceptRefs.label: Sinai derivation: general principle from Moses's preparation

misconceptions.misconception: Reish Lakish has no source for sequestering since he rejects the inauguration.
misconceptions.correction: He derives from Sinai: Moses covered by cloud for six days before being called establishes a general principle that entry into the Divine Presence requires preparation. His problem is only that Sinai gives six days while the mishna requires seven.

visualizableElements.item: Mishna text with 'mafrishin' (for the KG) and 'matkin' (for the backup) highlighted - the single-word difference that proves sequestering is not indispensable
Sinai timeline: Day 1-6 (cloud covers Moses) -> Day 7 (God calls Moses) = paradigm for entering Divine Presence

quizSeeds.question: What is Reish Lakish's formal challenge to deriving YK sequestering from the inauguration?
quizSeeds.answer: If YK is derived from the inauguration, then all inauguration details should be obligatory for YK - making failure to observe any of them invalidate the service. But the mishna shows sequestering is not invalidating (the backup priest is designated but not separated).

quizSeeds.question: How does Reish Lakish's Sinai derivation work, and what is its immediate problem?
quizSeeds.answer: Moses was covered in cloud for six days before God called him on the seventh - proving that entering the Divine Presence requires six days of preparation. The immediate problem: our mishna requires seven days, not six. (The resolution - R. Yehuda b. Beteira's concern for niddah impurity - appears on 4a.)

alternateAngles: The 'matkin vs. mafrishin' argument illustrates how Talmudic reasoning treats verbal precision as legally significant evidence. If the mishna intended 'separate' for the backup priest, it would have said so. The use of a different word is intentional and legally meaningful.

glossary.en: Take for yourself / Make for yourself. R. Yoshiya holds these phrases signal personal property; R. Yonatan holds they signal communal property but express God's preference for Moses's personal contribution. The Gemara ultimately bypasses the dispute by finding explicit textual contrasts in the specific passages.

A rabbinic stringency beyond the letter of the law, not derived from a Torah verse. The heifer sequestering is a maaleh added to compensate for the Sages' permissiveness in allowing a tvul yom to perform the heifer ritual. It looks identical to the YK sequestering in practice but differs in legal force.

Designate/prepare vs. remove/separate. The mishna's use of 'matkinim' (not 'mafrishin') for the backup priest proves that sequestering is not indispensable - if it were, the backup would also be required to separate. Single-word precision in mishnaic language carries legal weight.

The camp of the Divine Presence - the site of the Revelation at Sinai, and by extension the Holy of Holies on Yom Kippur. Reish Lakish's Sinai derivation creates a general principle: anyone entering the Machaneh Shekhinah requires prior preparation (six days according to Sinai, seven according to the mishna following R. Yehuda b. Beteira).

rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies 'aseh lecha': it is like 'make yourself two silver trumpets' - a personal command to Moses. He then introduces 'kiveychol' (as it were): 'I have heard that...'
Rashi explains the force of 'kiveychol': if the community could atone on behalf of an individual, God would want that from them - the word 'kiveychol' softens this by attributing a divine preference.
Rashi explains why the Sages used 'kiveychol': it is a difficult thing to say that God is displeased with Israel, so they say 'as it were, it is as if one could say so.'
Rashi notes that 'kiveychol' appears throughout the Talmud in this same softening function - wherever a statement about God would be too stark if said directly.
Rashi glosses 'when they do God's will': when Israel obeys, the work is called by their name - the verse credits them with the construction.
Rashi continues: when Israel fails to do God's will, the verse credits Moses alone with building the Ark.
Rashi notes that 'until here they disagree' is a resolution of the dispute, not a continuation of it - marking where the Gemara moves from the tannaitic position to its own analysis.

Rashi explains 'why does the Torah need the extra phrase kach lecha?': the verse already established that Israel must bring a goat kid - why add a separate command to Aaron with different language? The language-change is itself significant.
Rashi explains: the verse deviated from the formulation it began with, and that deviation is meaningful - it signals a different property status for Aaron's bull.
Rashi glosses the offering profile on Yom Kippur: there is a bull-chatat and a ram-olah as the personal offering of the Kohen Gadol.
Rashi continues: the personal offering of the KG on Yom Kippur consists of a bull-chatat and a ram-olah - this is the pattern derived from the inauguration.
Rashi explains the connection: Leviticus 16:3 ('With this Aaron shall come into the Sanctuary') refers to the eighth day of the inauguration, which also involved a calf-chatat and a ram-olah.
Rashi confirms: on the eighth day of the inauguration, there was also a calf-chatat and a ram-olah - the profile that Rav Ashi uses for the exclusion.
Rashi glosses Ravina's criterion: 'service restricted to the Kohen Gadol' - on Yom Kippur the entire service is reserved for the KG specifically.
Rashi identifies the inauguration parallel: on the eighth day, 'Aaron drew near to the altar' (Lev 9) - a service also restricted to the High Priest.
Rashi cites the verse: Leviticus 9, 'and Aaron drew near to the altar' - establishing the inauguration precedent for KG-restricted service.
Rashi explains Ravina's alternative: 'initial service' means we derive Yom Kippur as the first service imposed on the KG in communal public offerings...
...from the eighth day of the inauguration, which was the very first such service performed by a High Priest in all of history.
Rashi continues Ravina's alternative: Yom Kippur is the first service imposed on the KG with communal obligations on that original Yom Kippur.
Rashi identifies the inauguration side of the parallel: the eighth day of the inauguration was the first service ever performed by a High Priest - the original instance of priestly service.
Rashi completes the parallel: the eighth day was the first such High Priest service that had ever taken place in the world.
Rashi adds a qualification: Moses's seven days of service before the inauguration are not counted as priestly service - Moses served in a plain white garment, not the full priestly vestments.
Rashi explains Ravina's second version: 'initial service in the place' - Yom Kippur requires service before the Holy of Holies...
...and on the first Yom Kippur, this was the first time the Holy of Holies was ever entered for service - the inaugural service 'in the place' itself.
Rashi continues: the eighth day of the inauguration was the inaugural service at the outer altar, which had never been used before that day.
Rashi: before the eighth day, no one had ever entered the space to perform service there - it was literally the first such service in that location.
Rashi adds: the eighth day was the first time service was performed at the outer altar, matching Yom Kippur's status as the first time service was performed in the Holy of Holies.
Rashi notes: even if we consider Moses's service during the seven inauguration days, that service is not counted as priestly service...
...because Moses wore a plain white garment, not the full priestly vestments. Only service performed in the priestly vestments counts as priestly service in the formal sense.
Rashi continues: Moses's inauguration service in a plain white garment was a special temporary measure - it did not establish the category of priestly service.
Rashi explains: Moses's special status was valid only for that temporary period; regular priestly service must be in the full vestments.
Rashi completes: Moses's white garment (plain linen, not consecrated vestments) was only temporarily valid - 'and it was only made valid for that moment.'
Rashi explains R. Yochanan's position: he does not expound two cases from 'as has been done on this day' - he reads the whole phrase as referring to a single case (sequestering before Yom Kippur).
Rashi confirms: when R. Yochanan cites 'to do, to atone,' he does not split this into two separate derivations - he reads it as a unified reference to Yom Kippur service.
Rashi continues: R. Yochanan derives only one obligation of sequestering from this verse - the Yom Kippur sequestering. The heifer sequestering is for him a rabbinic maaleh only.
Rashi explains 'maaleh': because the Sages were lenient about the heifer ritual (permitting a tvul yom to perform it), they enacted compensating stringencies.
Rashi continues: because of the leniency granted to the tvul yom (who intentionally becomes impure and then immerses), the Sages added many extra requirements to the heifer service.
Rashi glosses 'from where did you derive this?': meaning, the seven-day sequestering before Yom Kippur - where in the Torah is this obligation found?
Rashi introduces the overspecification challenge: 'if so, just as all inauguration details are indispensable.' The detailed answer to this challenge is developed later at 5a.
Rashi cites the source for the indispensability principle: as we derive later in the tractate that all inauguration details are indispensable (Yoma 5a).

Rashi explains 'does not teach mafrishin': if the backup priest requires formal separation before potentially serving, the mishna would have said 'they also separate him.' It doesn't - proving the replacement can serve without having been separated.

Rashi makes the halakhic implication explicit: if a flaw occurs in the KG and the backup must serve, that backup serves without any seven-day separation - proving separation does not invalidate the YK service.
Rashi clarifies: since the replacement serves validly without prior separation, separation cannot be an indispensable condition. The mishna's word choice 'matkin' proves this.
Rashi introduces Reish Lakish's alternative: 'from Sinai' - we find that Moses withdrew before entering God's presence (the Machaneh Shekhinah).
Rashi continues Reish Lakish's Sinai derivation: the six days Moses was covered in cloud establish the general paradigm of preparation before entering the Divine Presence.
Rashi completes: the Sinai paradigm means that anyone entering the Machaneh Shekhinah requires six days of prior preparation - the cloud-covering served as that preparation for Moses.
Rashi clarifies 'vaykhasehu' (and it covered him): the pronoun refers to Moses, not the mountain - Moses was the one covered by the cloud for six days.
Rashi references R. Yehuda b. Beteira, whose position explains why the mishna requires seven rather than six days. This is discussed later in the tractate chapter (6a).
Rashi's final line on 3b: 'lest he become impure' - the word continues on the next page. Reish Lakish's answer to the seven-vs-six challenge begins on 4a.
